turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I have the opinion of the Court this morning in case 15-1391, Expressions Hair Design versus Schneiderman. Each time a customer pays for an item with a credit card, the merchant selling that item must pay a transaction fee to the company that issued the credit card. The petitioners in this case are New York Merchants who say that these transaction fees cost them tens of thousands of dollars every year. Now, rather than increase their prices across the board to absorb these costs, the merchants want to pass the fees along only to their customers who choose to use credit cards. Now, there are two ways they could do this. First they could charge higher prices to credit card users, a credit card surcharge or second, they could lower prices to customers who use cash or cash discount. The merchants who brought this case want to use the former approach imposing a surcharge for credit. They want to do this to make it clear to their customers that they, the merchants, are not the bad guys. It is the credit card companies that are making them pay more, use of credit card, pay a surcharge. New York however, has a law prohibiting credit card surcharges. The question before us is whether that law unconstitutionally regulates speech. Specifically, what the merchants want to do is post one cash price and then post an additional credit card surcharge expressed either as a dollars and cents additional amount or a percentage increase. For example, Petitioner, Expressions Hair Design might wish to post a sign outside its salon reading haircuts $10, we add 3% surcharge if you pay by credit card, or Petitioner Brooklyn Farmacy & Soda Fountain might list one of the sundaes on its menu as costing $10 with a 30 cent surcharge for credit card users. Now, the litigating parties all agree that New York’s law would prohibit them from doing this. The merchants argue that in doing so, New York’s law violates the First Amendment. The court that decided this case below, the Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit based in New York ruled that the law did not violate the First Amendment. In fact, that Court said that the law did not implicate the First Amendment at all because it regulated only conduct, not speech. We have said that laws that regulate prices are conduct, not speech regulations. A law that dictates that all New York delis can charge only $10 for their sandwiches just tells the deli owners how much they can collect each time they sell a sandwich. Now, of course, there will be in effect on speech. The waiters and waitresses cannot say that the sandwiches cost something else and the law regulates what deli owners may say on their signs. But we have ruled that such an incidental effect on speech does not trigger First Amendment concerns. To cite another example, it is illegal for competitors to fix prices and they are not protected by the First Amendment simply because they speak to agree on a price. But we think New York’s law is different. The law tells merchants nothing about the amount they are allowed to collect from a cash or credit card payer. Sellers are free to charge $10 dollars for cash and $9.70, $10 or $10.30 or any other amount for credit. What the law does regulate is how sellers may communicate their prices. A merchant who wants to charge $10 for cash and $10.30 for credit may not convey that price anyway he pleases. He is not free to say $10 with a 3% credit card surcharge or $10 dollars plus 30 cents for credit. Instead, if the merchant wishes to post only one sticker price, he must display $10.30 as that price. Because New York’s law regulates communication of prices rather than prices themselves, we hold today that the law regulates speech. Because the court below concluded that the law regulated only conduct, not speech, it did not view the law as even triggering First Amendment scrutiny and did not conduct any analysis of whether the law would survive such scrutiny. We generally refrained from deciding questions that the Courts below did not consider. As we often say, we are a Court of review, not first view. So that means that the Court of Appeals has some more work to do. We send the case back to that Court so it can analyze in the first instance now that it knows New York’s law regulates speech and not just conduct, whether the law is a permissible regulation of speech under the First Amendment. The judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit is vacated and the case is remanded for further proceedings. Justice Breyer has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment. Justice Sotomayor has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment in which Justice Alito has joined.
